summary: Gruber criticizes NPR's framing of Trump's TikTok executive order, arguing that the order doesn't 'pause' the law but merely directs the Justice Department not to enforce it — a crucial distinction. He draws an analogy to a mayor telling police not to enforce a city council law, likening the situation to banana republic governance. Gruber emphasizes that the bipartisan PAFACA law passed with overwhelming majorities in both chambers, and that Trump is asserting a de facto post-hoc veto power in violation of the Constitution's Article II Section 3. He frames this as a test of whether Republican lawmakers will accept executive overreach against their own legislation.
key_points: Executive orders cannot pause laws — they can only direct non-enforcement, and media framing that conflates the two is dangerously misleading
Trump's action amounts to a de facto after-the-fact veto over a law passed with overwhelming bipartisan support (360-58 in the House, 79-18 in the Senate)
This behavior is consistent with how Trump said he would govern, making it neither surprising nor any less constitutionally problematic
Republican senators Cotton and Ricketts, who championed PAFACA, now face a defining test of whether they'll accept executive overreach against their own legislation
money_quotes: The order does not pause the law, because executive orders can't 'pause' laws. The order pauses enforcement of the law.
The name of the town in my allegory above is Banana Republicville. The order doesn't change the law; it says to ignore the law.
Give him credit: this is exactly how he said he'd act (or, perhaps, to choose a more provocative verb, rule) if re-elected.
Let's not mince words or pretend for a moment this is even vaguely normal.
Trump is asserting a de facto after-the-fact veto power over a law that was already passed, in direct contravention of the plain language of Article II Section 3 of the Constitution.
Let's not mince words on this either: this man is a proud ignoramus. He relishes his ignorance.
key_insight: Media outlets undermine democratic accountability when they frame executive non-enforcement of a law as 'pausing' it, obscuring the constitutional violation at the heart of the action.
